~ * +

But—hardened sinners that we are—we
will probably see our folly out. Our claims
to being Nationalists must be laid down,
albeit with a sigh and weariness of heart,
‘Alas | that in our old age, as a supposedly
‘National institution, such contumely should
be poured upon us. We must, hereafter,
sunder ourselves from our Nationalist aspira-
tions, and henceforth content ourselves
with merely Netional ones. The tribula-
tion is heavy one, but what can we do but
grin and bear it?

~~ ~ ~

Last week we referred to a letter on the
question of Gaelic Games in the Colleges,
which appeared in a Dublin newspaper
above the signature of “A Student”, The
student aforesaid has made bold to direct a
further communication to us upon the
subject, abusing us roundly, but with a
singular lack of thought he bas omitted
pacticulars of identity. As he might
be a “Nationalist” in disguise, and
as these are sticring times, we offer
no apology for having a pardonable
curiosity on the subject. Should he think
well of it, we will publish his letter —abuse
and all—and his name, or other distinguish-
ing marks, need not pass out editorial
sanctum, "We hope he will accept this
sporting offer of a re-play.

DUBLIN NOTES.

‘At Croke Park on Sunday the Faughs-
Rapps. match was very well contested.
The Rapps. forwards were very weak
and never seemed to be able to get beyond
Cleary and ‘Boland, Faughs were the
superior team jin the second half, and scor-
ing two goals and a point qualified to meet
Davis in the final. The winners played a
very effective game and deserved their win,
their outstanding players being Mockler,
John Cleary, Boland and Hogan,

Parnells and Emeralds played their semi~
final next, the former being without Dick
Manifold and “Toby” Letmon, The
breeze favoured Emeralds in the second
period, and, as the result of good wing
play, they kept pressing and made the
scores level. ‘The football was not up to
championship standard, being very scraggy
and lacking finish, and Synnott and Carey
for Emeralds, and Mick Manitold!for Par-
nells were the only players to do them-
selves justice.

The junior hurling semi-final between
Davis and Lusk was the third match, and
some very good play was witnested, the

MEATH,

By the death of Mick” Newman, th-
Meath Gaelic Athletic Association has suse
tained a loss, the full extent of which may.
never probably be known. He was snatched
away in hisearly manhood with such appal-
ling suddenness that the first reports of bis
decease were regarded by all who knew
him on. the Gaelic field with amazement
and incredulity.» And well they might be.
As a footballer he was one of the best we
had; a8 a Gael’he had few equals. Al-
ways with’a ready smile, his face reRected
the kindness and gentleness, of his noble
nature. And now he i gone, and his
fellow-Gaels will meet. him no more on
earth, Truly, those whom the gods love
die young.

Tt is a sad story, and all the sadder on
account of its startling suddenness. No
wonder the first accounts of his demise were
disbelieved. A few days ago—barely, i
deed a short week—and he was full of life
and robust vigour and chatted on the pros
pects of his county's success in the Leinster
Championship—and now he is .with God,
We baried him on Sunday last in the old
cemetery of the Black Church, and he
sleeps the long sleep in the tomb of his
fathers, » Sorrow, indeed,

Michacl Newman died at Navan Fever
Hospital of dyphtheria after a few day
ness. Several beautiful wreaths, including
‘ne from the Meath County Committee,
G.A.A., were laid on the grave, The
following delegation. attended from the
County Committee—Seaghan Mac Na
midhe, chairman ; Matthew OYToole,
vice-chairman ; T. J. Hewitt and J. Hig-
gins,
